citations.narrative:
An outdoor housing facility did not protect an animal from injury. On 3/9/2026, the facility states an adult male coyote was
found to have sustained injuries to both right legs. The facility believes this was caused when wild coyotes were able to
interact with the animal through the metal chain link fencing of the enclosure. The incident caused a section of fencing to
be noticeably bent outwards. The facility did seek veterinary care for the animal but the injuries sustained did result in the
animal being euthanized.
The facility believes the wild coyotes were able enter the facility by traversing up the ravine behind the enclosure. While
there is a fence between the ravine and the enclosure, it is not continuous and ends near the double entry gate to the
enclosure and this is the area the facility states they feel the wild coyotes gained entry into the facility. Failure to protect
animals from injury can result in stress, pain, and injury to regulated animals. Correct by ensuring that the facility is
constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The outdoor housing facilities
shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury. Correct by 11-MAY-
2026.

The facility houses an Eastern Fox Squirrel in an outdoor enclosure and does not have a perimeter fence on several
sides. The facility does have a perimeter fence variance but this species is not covered in the approved variance. Failure
to provide an appropriate perimeter fence may result in animal escape or allow persons or other animals to interact with
the regulated animals. All outdoor housing facilities must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that is of sufficient height to
keep animals and unauthorized person out. Fences less than 8 feet high for potentially dangerous animals, such as but
not limited to, large felines (e.g., lions, tigers, leopards, cougars, etc.), bears, wolves, rhinoceros, and elephants, or less
than 6 feet high for other animals must be approved in writing by the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that
it protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals and unauthorized persons from going through it or under it and
having contact with the animals in the facility, and so that it can function as a secondary containment system for the
animals in the facility. It must be of sufficient distance from the outside of the primary enclosure to prevent physical
contact between animals inside the enclosure and animals or persons outside the perimeter fence. Such fences less than
3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure must be approved in writing by the Administrator. Correct by ensuring that all
species requiring a perimeter fence are completely enclosed within an adequate perimeter fence that meets all
requirements. Alternatively, an updated variance may be requested in writing and sent to the APHIS Administrator.
Correct by 27-MAY-2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a Facility Representative.
The perimeter fence variance is currently under review.
n

narrative:
3.125(a)  Critical
   
Facilities, general. 
An outdoor housing facility did not protect an animal from injury. On 3/9/2026, the facility states an adult male coyote was 
found to have sustained injuries to both right legs. The facility believes this was caused when wild coyotes were able to 
interact with the animal through the metal chain link fencing of the enclosure. The incident caused a section of fencing to 
be noticeably bent outwards. The facility did seek veterinary care for the animal but the injuries sustained did result in the 
animal being euthanized.  
 
The facility believes the wild coyotes were able enter the facility by traversing up the ravine behind the enclosure. While 
there is a fence between the ravine and the enclosure, it is not continuous and ends near the double entry gate to the 
enclosure and this is the area the facility states they feel the wild coyotes gained entry into the facility. Failure to protect 
animals from injury can result in stress, pain, and injury to regulated animals. Correct by ensuring that the facility is 
constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. The outdoor housing facilities 
shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury. Correct by 11-MAY-
2026. 
3.127(d) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
The facility houses an Eastern Fox Squirrel in an outdoor enclosure and does not have a perimeter fence on several 
sides. The facility does have a perimeter fence variance but this species is not covered in the approved variance. Failure 
to provide an appropriate perimeter fence may result in animal escape or allow persons or other animals to interact with 
the regulated animals. All outdoor housing facilities must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that is of sufficient height to 
keep animals and unauthorized person out. Fences less than 8 feet high for potentially dangerous animals, such as but 
not limited to, large felines (e.g., lions, tigers, leopards, cougars, etc.), bears, wolves, rhinoceros, and elephants, or less 
than 6 feet high for other animals must be approved in writing by the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that 
it protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals and unauthorized persons from going through it or under it and 
having contact with the animals in the facility, and so that it can function as a secondary containment system for the 
animals in the facility. It must be of sufficient distance from the outside of the primary enclosure to prevent physical 
contact between animals inside the enclosure and animals or persons outside the perimeter fence. Such fences less than 
3 feet in distance from the primary enclosure must be approved in writing by the Administrator. Correct by ensuring that all

species requiring a perimeter fence are completely enclosed within an adequate perimeter fence that meets all 
requirements. Alternatively, an updated variance may be requested in writing and sent to the APHIS Administrator. 
Correct by 27-MAY-2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a Facility Representative. 
 
The perimeter fence variance is currently under review.

species.common: RED FOX (INCLUDES SILVER FOX & CROSS FOX)
